title: Attacking Chess
description: Learn to attack the king through annotated master games — calculate lines, sacrifice material, and finish with unstoppable kingside attacks.
subject: chess

chapters.title: Attacking Chess
chapters.description: Sacrifice, development, and punishing materialistic play.

lessons.title: Caro-Kann Kingside Attack
lessons.description: FM ChessWhiz57 vs WGM Silvermist15 — rip open the black kingside with pawn storms and piece infiltration in a Caro-Kann.

steps.title: Attacking the Caro-Kann king
steps.body:
White builds a classic **Exchange Caro-Kann** setup, then launches a kingside attack. 

At each **STOP**, find White's best continuation on the board — then read the commentary. Strong attackers calculate **lines**, not isolated moves.

steps.title: The Exchange Caro-Kann setup
steps.description: White develops quickly, occupies the center, and reaches a familiar Caro-Kann position.

moveAnnotations.4: The Exchange Variation — White gives up the e-pawn for quick development and central pressure.
moveAnnotations.8: Ne5 attacks the f7 pawn and forces Black to block with …Nbd7.
moveAnnotations.14: White completes development and connects the rooks. A common Caro-Kann setup has been reached.
moveAnnotations.21: We have arrived at a common Caro-Kann opening setup.

8.label: Ne5 — hits f7
8.detail: The knight attacks the **f7** pawn and forces Black to block with …Nbd7.

steps.title: Preparing Ba6 and clogging the diagonal
steps.description: White plays Qe2 to keep the d3 bishop alive. Black responds with …g6 to block the light-squared bishop.

moveAnnotations.0: Qe2 eyes **a6** along the e2–a6 diagonal — preparing **Ba6** to keep the d3 bishop alive. An important move that will come into play later on.
moveAnnotations.1: Black clogs up the diagonal to try and prevent an attack on the kingside. Find a continuation for White.

steps.question: Black just played …g6. Find the best continuation for White.

0.g4: g4 is part of the plan later. Find something more threatening.
0.Bf4: Not bad, but try and find a blow to the king.
0.Bh6: A great move. Find another move that attacks the king.

boardAnnotations.label: h4 — the spearhead
boardAnnotations.detail: The h-pawn lunges forward, threatening **h5** to crack open the black king's cover. Speed matters more than the pawn.

boardAnnotations.label: g6 is the hook
boardAnnotations.detail: After **h5**, the h-pawn strikes g6. Black can't hold the kingside closed — lines open straight toward the king.

boardAnnotations.label: Bishop already aimed
boardAnnotations.detail: The d3 bishop sits on the **b1–h7 diagonal**. The moment the kingside opens, it points right at h7.

steps.explanation: h4! A great move. White pushes the h-pawn to hook the g-pawn and rip open the b1–h7 diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
steps.successMessage: h4 — start the kingside pawn storm!

steps.question: Black played …Nh5. Find the best continuation for White.

0.g4: A great move. Find another move that attacks the king.
0.Nf3: Retreating the knight gives Black time to consolidate. Keep attacking.

steps.explanation: Bh6! White's bishop lands on h6, eyeing the f8 rook. Black must respond — now find a way to rip open the kingside.
steps.successMessage: Bh6 — dominate the dark squares!

steps.question: Black played …Re8. Find a way to rip open the black kingside.

0.h5: Too early — drive the knight from h5 with a pawn break on the kingside first.
0.Qe3: The queen will join later. A pawn break on the kingside is the forcing move.
0.Nf3: Too passive. Rip open the kingside with a pawn break first.

steps.explanation: g4! Good move. White drives the knight away and prepares to open the h-file. Now show that you understand it by following up.
steps.successMessage: g4 — drive the knight and open lines!

steps.question: Black retreated …Ng7. Find White's best continuation.

0.g5: g5 is premature — open the kingside pawn cover before pushing further.
0.Qe3: Bring the queen in later. Open the kingside pawn cover first.
0.Bf4: Too slow. Open the kingside pawn cover first.

steps.explanation: h5! Excellent. White rips open the black kingside with his pawns.
steps.successMessage: h5 — rip open the kingside!

steps.question: After …Bxe5 dxe5 Nc5 — what does Black want to do here? With that in mind, find the best continuation.

0.Nd4: Black threatens …Ba6 — save the attacked bishop before continuing the attack.
0.Qe3: The queen will join the attack, but retreat the bishop first — Black threatens …Ba6.
0.b4: Too slow. Black threatens …Ba6 — retreat the bishop before it gets trapped.

steps.explanation: Bc2! Indeed, this is the computer recommended move. White retreats the bishop to preserve it and keep the attack going. Remember: we cannot just consider our own ideas — we must also consider what our opponent intends to do.
steps.successMessage: Bc2 — save the bishop and keep attacking!

steps.question: Black played …Bb7. Find a way to advance the pieces forward and attack the black kingside.

0.Nb5: Black may break with …d4 — block that idea and improve the knight first.
0.Qe3: The queen belongs in the attack, but the knight has a precise jump that blocks Black's plans.
0.f3: Too early — improve the knight and block Black's plans on d4 first.

steps.explanation: Nd4! Excellent choice. White blocks Black's potential d4 idea to open up the black bishop and prepares the Nb5–Nd6 maneuver to infiltrate the black side.
steps.successMessage: Nd4 — block d4 and infiltrate!

steps.question: Black played …Ne4. What is Black threatening? How do we stop it?

0.Qe3: Qe3 ignores the knight on e4 — drive it back with a pawn thrust first.
0.Nxe4: Nxe4 allows …Bxe4 with complications. A pawn thrust is cleaner.
0.Re3: Too slow. Stop the knight with a pawn thrust that opens lines.

0.Bf4: Bf4 defends the pawn on e5 but is too slow. As an attacker, we rarely make backwards moves — find the forward pawn thrust that gains tempo.

steps.explanation: f3! White drives the knight back and keeps the attack rolling. Bf4 was playable but too slow — as an attacker, avoid backwards moves when a forward thrust is available.
steps.successMessage: f3 — forward, not backwards!

steps.question: After …Ba6 Qe3 Nc5 — find a way to bring more pieces into the attack.

0.Rh1: Too early — tuck the king first so the rook lift on the h-file is safe.
0.Qh3: Too direct. Tuck the king first so you can lift the rook with safety.
0.Re3: Re3 is passive. Get the king out of the way before the h-file assault.

steps.explanation: Kg2! An excellent choice, and indeed the computer's top move. White prepares Rh1 to open up the h-file and crash in on the kingside. Bring more pieces into the attack.
steps.successMessage: Kg2 — prepare Rh1 and bring in the heavy pieces!

steps.question: Black played …Qe7. What should White play?

0.Qh3: Qh3 is strong but bring the heavy piece onto the h-file first — that opens it with maximum force.
0.Nf5: Nf5 is interesting but doubling rooks on the h-file is the direct way to crash through.
0.Re3: Too slow. Bring the heavy piece onto the h-file immediately.

steps.explanation: Rh1! White doubles on the h-file and prepares the final breakthrough. Black tries …f5 to block — but White has a combination ready.
steps.successMessage: Rh1 — the h-file is yours!

steps.question: DIFFICULT: Black played …f5. Find a combination that wins material for White.

0.gxf5: gxf5 opens the g-file but there's a forcing pawn capture that wins material first.
0.Nxf5: Nxf5 is not as strong — look for a pawn capture that wins material by force.
0.Qh3: Qh3 misses the tactical shot. Look for a forcing pawn capture that wins material.

2.Qh3: A forcing pawn capture opens the h-file — do that before bringing the queen in.
2.Nf5: Keep calculating — ripping open the kingside with a pawn capture is next.

4.Qh3: Win material on g7 with tempo before bringing the queen in.
4.Rh6: Not yet — win material on g7 first.

steps.explanation: exf6! A brilliant combination. White sacrifices the e-pawn to open lines, then hxg6 and Bxg7 win material while keeping the attack alive.
steps.successMessage: Stunning combination — material is yours!

steps.question: Black captured …Qxg7. Find the best continuation.

0.Qh3: Qh3 is strong but the rook has a lift on the kingside that crashes through immediately.
0.Bxg7: The bishop is already gone. Lift the rook into the attack on the kingside.
0.Nf5: Too slow. The rook lift wins by force.

steps.explanation: Rh6! White brings the rook in and crashes in. Black has no way to stop Rxg6 and now loses. The attack finishes in style.
steps.successMessage: Rh6 — crash through on the kingside!

steps.title: The attack finishes
steps.description: White's pieces swarm the exposed king — a model kingside attack in the Caro-Kann.

moveAnnotations.0: Black recaptures on g7 — but White's attack is unstoppable.
moveAnnotations.1: Qg5+ — the queen joins with check, driving the king forward.
moveAnnotations.3: Qf6+ keeps the king on the run.
moveAnnotations.5: Bh7#! White delivers checkmate. A crushing kingside attack — the Caro-Kann king never found safety.

sections.title: How Do I Launch a Good Attack?

lessons.title: How Do I Launch a Good Attack?
lessons.description: Build board vision, learn when Ng5 works, and practice calculating every reply before you commit to an attack.

steps.title: What is a good attack?
steps.body:
The most important question in attacking chess: **what makes an attack good?**

A good attack isn't just a flashy move, or a clever idea. These must be backed by coordinated pieces, calculated lines, and timing. We'll start with a basic version of an attack and understand whether it is good or not.

steps.title: Board vision: seeing threats before they land
steps.body:
Strong players don't just look at the piece they're moving — they scan the **entire board**.

Every move changes the position. You need to ask: after I move, what can my opponent do? What squares are newly attacked? What pieces are now undefended?

The three things to scan every move:
1. **Checks** — is the king attacked?
2. **Captures** — can any piece be taken for free?
3. **Threats** — what is my opponent threatening to do next?

These are also called **candidate moves** — the forcing moves you must consider first.
steps.analogy: Think of board vision like peripheral vision in a sport. A great basketball player doesn't stare at the ball — they see the whole court. A great chess player doesn't stare at one piece — they see every threat on the board.

steps.question: What should you always check FIRST when considering a move?

options.text: Checks, captures, and threats — in that order
options.explanation: Correct. Forcing moves (checks first, then captures, then threats) narrow your candidate list and ensure you don't miss a tactic.

options.text: The move that develops the most pieces
options.explanation: Development matters, but only after you've verified your king is safe and there's no immediate tactic to play or defend.

options.text: The move your opponent least expects
options.explanation: Surprise has value, but not at the cost of missing a forced sequence. Candidate moves — checks, captures, threats — come first.

options.text: The move that controls the center
options.explanation: Center control is important in the long run, but a forcing tactic beats positional goals every time.

steps.question: White to move. Find a forcing move.

0.Nxe5: Nxe5 loses the piece after black immediately recaptures.
0.d3: A great quiet developing move. Now find a more forcing move.
0.O-O: A great developing move as well. Find a more forcing move.

steps.explanation: Ng5 attacks f7 — the weakest point in Black's position, defended only by the king. Losing it would lead to an unstoppable double threat: Nxf7 winning the rook. The candidate move check reveals it immediately.
steps.successMessage: Nice eye! Ng5 exploits the weakest square in Black's position.

boardAnnotations.label: Ng5 — attacks f7
boardAnnotations.detail: The knight jumps to g5, attacking **f7** — defended only by the king. If Black loses f7, **Nxf7** wins the rook.

steps.title: Does this move work?
steps.body: You found **Ng5** as a one-move tactic — but is one move enough? Before you commit, ask: do I have **enough pieces** in the attack? And if Black doesn't fold, **how far have I calculated**?

steps.title: Deep Calculation: Beyond The First Move
steps.body:
Ng5 looks premature if you only see one move — but what if you've **calculated the whole line**?

In the **Two Knights Defense**, after **Ng5 d5 exd5 Nxd5**, White sometimes has a stunning follow-up. Let's see whether deep calculation redeems the knight jump.

steps.title: The Ng5 gambit line
steps.description: Step through Ng5, …d5, exd5, and …Nxd5 — the pawn grab that opens the position.

moveAnnotations.0: Ng5! — attacking f7 and the pawn on e5. Aggressive, but now Black has a say.
moveAnnotations.1: …d5! — the standard counter. Black strikes in the center instead of passively defending f7.
moveAnnotations.2: exd5 — White captures. Black now has several ways to recapture.
moveAnnotations.3: …Nxd5 — Black grabs the pawn. If you calculated deeply enough, you have a thunderous reply.

0.label: Ng5 — attacks f7
0.detail: The knight attacks **f7** and the pawn on e5. Aggressive, but now Black has a say.

steps.question: Black played …Nxd5, grabbing the pawn. If you calculated the Ng5 line deeply enough, you have a thunderous reply. Find it.

0.Nxe5: Nxe5 is not the crash-through move. Look at the undefended f7 pawn — the whole Ng5 idea was pointing there.
0.Bc4: The bishop is already on c4. Look at the undefended f7 pawn — that's where the attack points.
0.d4: Too slow. Tear open the king on f7 and win back material with interest.

steps.explanation: Nxf7! White sacrifices the knight to rip open the f7 square. After …Kxf7 Qf3+ the king is driven out and White's attack crashes through. One forcing move often has a thunderous follow-up you only see if you keep calculating.
steps.successMessage: Nxf7! — you calculated the crash-through line!

boardAnnotations.label: Nxf7! — rip open f7
boardAnnotations.detail: White sacrifices on **f7**, tearing open the king's cover. After …Kxf7 **Qf3+** drives the king out.

steps.title: One brilliant line isn't enough
steps.body: Nxf7! is spectacular when Black plays …Nxd5 — but that isn't Black's only reply. Find Black's best response.

steps.title: Black's refutation
steps.description: Same Ng5 gambit — but Black hits the bishop with …Na5 instead of grabbing on d5.

moveAnnotations.0: Ng5! — the same committal jump. White is betting on a calculated attack.
moveAnnotations.1: …d5! — Black counters in the center again.
moveAnnotations.2: exd5 — White grabs the pawn. Now find Black's best reply — not …Nxd5 this time.

steps.question: Black to move. White's Ng5 attack looks scary — but you've prepared the refutation. Find Black's best move and follow through.

0.Nxd5: That's the line White was praying for — taking on d5 lets White rip open your king. Find the move that attacks the bishop on c4.
0.Ndb5: The knight hops to the rim but doesn't pressure c4. Attack the bishop on c4 and eye the d5 pawn.
0.Ne7: Ne7 is passive — hit the bishop on c4 and force White to prove the attack was sound.

2.Bd7: Bd7 blocks on the diagonal but wastes time — a pawn strike in the center is the forcing reply.

2.Nc6: Nc6 works too, but a pawn thrust in the center is the cleaner refutation — it damages White's structure and opens lines.

steps.explanation: …Na5 attacks the bishop on c4 — White's whole Ng5 idea depended on that piece. After Bb5+ c6 dxc6 bxc6, Black is down a pawn, but every piece is pointed at the center and ready to develop. White's bishop on b5 is awkward, the knight on g5 is offside, and the rest of White's army is still on the first rank.
steps.successMessage: …Na5 refutes the attack — White's pieces are tangled while yours spring into action!

steps.title: Why it still fails
steps.description: Black doesn't need to accept a gambit — just develop calmly, and Ng5 becomes a wasted move.

moveAnnotations.0: White tries Ng5 again — but without the forcing …Nxd5 line, it's just a committal jump.

steps.title: Precision over style
steps.body:
Attacking is **dynamic and dangerous**. Launching an attack costs committal moves — and failed sacrifices cost the game.

Ng5 can look brilliant when you've calculated **Nxf7**, but it fails when Black hits your bishop with …Na5 or simply develops with …d6.

**Don't go for style — go for precision.** Use **Deep Calculation** *and* **Calculate All Relevant Lines** before you commit.

Now you're ready for **Taking the initiative** — an Italian Game where White's attack is backed by real calculation and speedy development.

lessons.title: Taking the Initiative
lessons.description: Step through a classic Italian Game where White sacrifices material for a devastating attack — find the best moves at every critical moment.

steps.title: Attacking chess: lines, not moves
steps.body:
In attacking chess, **speed of development** and **king safety** matter more than counting pawns.

This lesson walks through a classic **Italian Game** where White sacrifices material for a devastating attack. At each **STOP**, you'll find White's best continuation on the board — then read the annotator's commentary.

Remember: strong players calculate **lines** (whole sequences), not isolated moves. Always ask: "Where does this lead?"

steps.title: The Italian Game unfolds
steps.description: Step through the opening. White develops quickly, strikes in the center with d4, and sets a trap after castling.

moveAnnotations.4: The classic Italian. White develops pieces into the center as fast as possible. This move order also allows White to castle the king out of the center — a great opening.
moveAnnotations.6: White uses its move-advantage and plays c3 to set up d4, taking control of the center as fast as possible. White has an extra tempo — use it to pressure Black as much as possible and take the initiative!
moveAnnotations.8: Boom! White strikes in the center with deadly force, attacking the e5 pawn and c5 bishop.
moveAnnotations.10: Admittedly e5 is a better move here to grab more space and attack the f6 knight — but for this lesson we study one specific line after cxd4.
moveAnnotations.13: With this combination Black seems to win a pawn while threatening another on c3. But White simply castles, preparing a deadly trap…
moveAnnotations.15: Black captures on c3 — a blunder! White has a devastating continuation. Before you step forward, get ready to find it.

steps.question: Black just played …Nxc3. Find White's best continuation.

0.Qd3: Too slow. The queen is hanging, but this move is not forcing enough.
0.Nd5: The knight has nowhere useful to go. There are no immediate threats.
0.Re1: Passive. Black is threatening …Nxc3 and …Nxe4 ideas — deal with the hanging queen first.

steps.explanation: Remember. In chess we always want **lines** calculated rather than moves. It is not good to just see one move ahead — you must see until you find yourself in a good spot. After …Bxc3 White has a stunning follow-up.
steps.successMessage: Correct — recapture and keep calculating the whole line!

steps.question: Black played …Bxc3. Find White's best move.

0.Qd3: Qd3 develops the queen but misses the tactical point. Look for a move that creates multiple threats at once against Black's king-side and the bishop on c3.
0.Re1: Re1 is logical development but too slow. Black's king is stuck in the center — find the forcing move that exploits that immediately.

0.Ba3: Great find! This is actually one of the best moves — White cuts off Black's castling possibilities while developing the bishop. If Black plays …Bxa1, then White follows with Re1+ Ne7 and Rxe7+, winning the black queen. For our purposes, try to find the other great move.

steps.explanation: Good move! Qb3 forks the bishop on c3 and the pawn on f7. White has sacrificed two pawns, but Black will quickly find out that material-oriented chess is poor chess. White's speedy development and Black's vulnerable king make for a very deadly combo.
steps.successMessage: Strong attacking move! Material is less important than king safety and development.

boardAnnotations.label: Qb3 — double attack
boardAnnotations.detail: The queen forks the bishop on **c3** and the pawn on **f7**.

steps.question: Black captured the rook on a1 with …Bxa1. What is White's best continuation?

0.Re1+: Re1+ allows Black to block with …Be6.
0.Bg5: Bg5 is strong later. Find a better continuation

steps.explanation: Bxf7+! White captures f7 to expose Black's king. After …Kf8 the attack continues with devastating effect — White's pieces swarm the exposed king while Black's extra rook is useless.
steps.successMessage: Attack the king!

boardAnnotations.label: Bxf7+ — king exposed
boardAnnotations.detail: The bishop captures on **f7**, ripping open the king's cover.

steps.question: Black played …Kf8. What is White's best continuation?

0.Qf3: Qf3 is playable but find a more precise continuation. Remember that we need to bring all the pieces into the attack.
0.Ne5: Ne5 is strong but we have some pieces that are left behind.
0.Re1: Strong idea. Bringing more pieces in. Is there a more forcing way?

steps.explanation: Bg5! White brings the bishop into the game with tempo, attacking the queen. Black's king on f8 has no safe haven and with every move we put more pressure on.
steps.successMessage: Bg5 — continue with force!

steps.title: Building the attack
steps.description: White continues the assault — the knight leaps to e5 and Black tries to defend with …d6.

moveAnnotations.0: Black tries to block the e5 square, but White's attack is far from over.
moveAnnotations.1: Ne5! The knight joins the attack, eyeing c6 and f7. Black's position is crumbling.
moveAnnotations.2: Black blocks the knight — but White has a stunning resource ready.

1.label: Ne5 — joins the attack
1.detail: The knight eyes **c6** and **f7**. Black's position is crumbling.

steps.question: Black played …d6. Can you find the best continuation?

0.Qf3: Qf3 is a great move, but there is a better one. Can you spot it?
0.Re1: Not bad. Is there a more devasting blow?
0.Rxa1: Rxa1 is fine, but why do we care about this bishop? Let's checkmate our opponent.

steps.explanation: Bg6!! This move deserves two exclamation marks. White threatens checkmate by opening up the square on f7 for the queen. White also threatens to swing the queen over to f3 for mate. In fact, mate is unstoppable. Bring all pieces into the attack.
steps.successMessage: Brilliant! Bg6 — mate is unstoppable.

steps.title: What if Black tries …d5?
steps.body:
The main line continues **14…dxe5 15.Qf7#** — instant checkmate.

But if Black tries **14…d5** instead, White has a beautiful forcing sequence.

steps.title: The …d5 sideline
steps.description: If Black blocks with …d5, White forces the king into the open with a series of checks and captures.

moveAnnotations.0: Black tries to block the center — but White's attack is too fast.
moveAnnotations.1: Qf3+! The queen joins with check, forcing Black's pieces into awkward squares.
moveAnnotations.4: Bxf5 wins the knight while keeping the attack going.
moveAnnotations.7: Bxe6+ drives the king forward — there is no escape.
moveAnnotations.9: Qf7+ keeps the king on the run.
moveAnnotations.11: Bxd5 — White has a huge material advantage.
moveAnnotations.12: Rhe8 — Black tries to defend, but White has mate in one.

steps.question: Can you find mate in 1?

0.Qe8: Qe8 is not checkmate — the king escapes. The knight can deliver a smothered-style mate from the c-file.
0.Bxe6: Bxe6 wins material but misses the immediate mate. Find the knight jump that seals every escape.
0.Qf5: Qf5 gives check but is not mate — the king can move. Find the knight jump that seals every escape.

steps.explanation: Nc4#! The knight delivers checkmate — the black king on d6 has no escape squares. A beautiful finish to the forcing line.
steps.successMessage: Nc4# — mate in one!

steps.title: The main line finishes
steps.description: Back to the main line — after Bg6, Black captures on e5 but White delivers instant checkmate.

moveAnnotations.0: Black captures the knight — but it is far too late.
moveAnnotations.1: Qf7# — Checkmate! White's queen lands on f7 with the bishop on g6 controlling escape squares. Black's material advantage meant nothing against White's attack. This is attacking chess.
